items.question: State the definition of GELU and name Φ(x).
items.answer: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), where Φ(x) is the CDF of the standard normal distribution N(0,1)—the probability that a standard normal draw is less than or equal to x.

items.question: Why did BERT choose GELU instead of ReLU in its FFN layers?
items.answer: GELU provides a smooth, differentiable gate that softly handles negative inputs and empirically worked well in deep Transformer stacks; ReLU's hard zero and kink at zero are less aligned with the residual FFN design BERT adopted from prior NLP scaling experiments.

items.question: Write the tanh approximation used in many GELU implementations and explain when exact erf matters.
items.answer: Common form: 0.5·x·(1 + tanh(√(2/π)·(x + 0.044715·x³))). Exact erf matters when you need bit-level parity across export paths, numerical audits, or when mixing frameworks that default to different approximations.

items.question: Compare the derivative behavior of GELU and ReLU near x = 0.
items.answer: ReLU has derivative 0 for x < 0, 1 for x > 0, and a subgradient ambiguity at 0. GELU's derivative is continuous: it blends Φ(x) and terms from the normal PDF, giving a well-defined slope near zero (~0.5 at x = 0 for the standard definition).
